addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
# Specifying and Verifying C Programs

You are an expert formal verification engineer specializing in the CBMC (C
Bounded Model Checker) tool.

Your task is to edit C programs to insert CBMC specifications (contracts) that
CBMC can verify.  Ideally, when you are done, CBMC should succeed when run on
each function, one-by-one.

You must produce strong specifications.

It may be OK if a few of the specifications you write do not verify, for two
reasons.  First, if a program is incorrect, CBMC will issue a warning.  Second,
CBMC cannot verify all correct C code.  Do not fix or otherwise change the C
code, except to insert specifications in it.

This `CLAUDE.md` file and directory `docs/` contain basic information about using CBMC.
The main documentation can be found at at https://diffblue.github.io/cbmc/index.html,
which includes a [User Guide](https://diffblue.github.io/cbmc/user_guide.html)
and [The CPROVER Manual](https://diffblue.github.io/cbmc/cprover-manual/index.html).
You can also search the web for more CBMC documentation.

## Tool Use

- **To run CBMC on a function**, run:

  ```sh
  run-cbmc --function <FUNCTION_NAME> \
                   --file <PATH_TO_C_FILE> \
                   [-I <PATH_TO_INCLUDE_DIR(S)>]...
  ```

  If verification succeeds, exits with status 0.
  If verification fails, exits with non-zero status and prints a possibly-truncated failure diagnostic to stdout.

  You should always prefer `run-cbmc` over invoking CBMC directly.

  **This tool can run for a long time, and that is expected — not a hang.**

- **To obtain a call graph of the functions in a file in JSON format**, run:

  ```sh
  construct-call-graph <PATH_TO_C_FILE>
  ```

  Prints the path to a newly written JSON file that is a sibling of the source file,
  For example, invoking `construct-call-graph /app/a/b/file.c` will print `/app/a/b/file-callgraph.json`

- **To obtain a reverse topological ordering of functions in a call graph, with all callees before their callers**:

  ```sh
  topological-order <PATH_TO_CALL_GRAPH_JSON>
  ```

  Prints function names callees-first, one per line.

## Rules

- Never hard-code any values into the specifications that are related to CBMC's command-line
  arguments (e.g., the `N` in `--partial-loops --unwind <N>`).
- Never attempt to fix a failing specification for a function more than 5 times.
- Do not attempt to verify `main` functions.
- Never re-run verification on previously-verified functions unless:
  - The specification has changed, or
  - The specification of a callee has changed, or
  - You need information from a callee's verification run to help verify a caller.
- You must not delete any scripts if you create and use them to help you.
- If you run into errors related to missing bodies or callee implementations, you may write a
  non-deterministic specification in a stub file.

1	# Frees Clauses
2	
3	A *frees clause* allows the user to specify a set of pointers that may be freed by a function or by the function it calls, transitively. A function contract may have zero or more frees clauses. When no clause is provided the empty set is used as default. Contracts can also have an empty frees clause. When more than one frees clause is given, the sets of pointers they contain are unioned together to yield a single set of pointers.
4	
5	## Syntax
6	
7	The clause has the following syntax (square brackets denote optional expressions `[` `]`):
8	
9	```
10	__CPROVER_frees([targets])
11	```
12	
13	Where `targets` has the following syntax:
14	
15	```
16	          targets ::= cond-target-group (';' cond-target-group)* ';'?
17	cond-target-group ::= (condition ':' )? target (',' target)*
18	           target ::= lvalue-expr
19	                    | __CPROVER_freeable(lvalue-expr)
20	```
21	
22	A frees clause target must be either:
23	
24	- an lvalue expression with a pointer type,
25	- a call to the built-in function `__CPROVER_freeable`
26	- a call to a user-defined side effect free and deterministic function returning the type `void` (itself containing direct or indirect calls to `__CPROVER_freeable` or to functions that call `__CPROVER_freeable`)
27	
28	### Example
29	
30	```c
31	int foo(char *arr1, char *arr2, size_t size)
32	__CPROVER_frees(
33	    // `arr1` is freeable only if the condition `size > 0 && arr1` holds
34	    size > 0 && arr1: arr1;
35	
36	    // `arr2` is always freeable
37	    arr2;
38	)
39	{
40	  if(size > 0 && arr1)
41	    free(arr1);
42	  free(arr2);
43	  return 0;
44	}
45	```
46	
47	## Semantics
48	
49	The set of pointers specified by the frees clause of the contract is interpreted at the function call-site where the contract is being checked or used to abstract a function call.
50	
51	### For contract checking
52	
53	When checking a contract against a function, each pointer that the function attempts to free gets checked for membership in the set of pointers specified by the *frees clause*.
54	
55	### For replacement of function calls by contracts
56	
57	When replacing a function call by a contract, each pointer of the *frees clause* gets non-deterministically freed between the evaluation of preconditions and before the evaluation of post-conditions.
58	
59	## Specifying parametric sets of freeable pointers using C functions
60	
61	Users can define parametric sets of freeable pointers by writing functions that return the `void` type and call (directly or indirectly) the built-in function `__CPROVER_freeable`:
62	
63	```c
64	void my_freeable_set(char **arr, size_t size)
65	{
66	  // The first 3 pointers are freeable
67	  // if the array is at least of size 3.
68	  if (arr && size > 3) {
69	    __CPROVER_freeable(arr[0]);
70	    __CPROVER_freeable(arr[1]);
71	    __CPROVER_freeable(arr[2]);
72	  }
73	}
74	```
75	
76	Calling the built-in function:
77	
78	```c
79	void __CPROVER_freeable(void *ptr);
80	```
81	
82	in the context of a frees clause specifies that `ptr` is freeable in that context.
83	
84	```c
85	void my_function(char **arr, size_t size)
86	__CPROVER_frees(
87	  // arr is considered freeable in the context of this clause.
88	  my_freeable_set(arr, size)
89	)
90	{
91	  // body ...
92	}
93	```
94	
95	## Frees clause related predicates
96	
97	The predicate:
98	
99	```c
100	__CPROVER_bool __CPROVER_is_freeable(void *ptr);
101	```
102	
103	can only be used in pre and post conditions, in contract checking or replacement modes. It returns `true` if and only if the pointer satisfies the preconditions of the `free` function from `stdlib.h`, that is if and only if the pointer points to a valid dynamically allocated object and has offset zero.
104	
105	The predicate:
106	
107	```c
108	__CPROVER_bool __CPROVER_was_freed(void *ptr);
109	```
110	
111	can only be used in post conditions and returns `true` if and only if the pointer was freed during the execution of the function under analysis.
112

1	# Assigns Clauses
2	
3	## Table of Contents
4	
5	- [Syntax](#syntax)
6	  - [Lvalue targets](#lvalue-targets)
7	  - [Object slice targets](#object-slice-targets)
8	  - [Function parameters](#function-parameters)
9	  - [Inductive data structures](#inductive-data-structures)
10	- [Semantics](#semantics)
11	  - [Contract Enforcement](#contract-enforcement)
12	  - [Contract Replacement](#contract-replacement)
13	- [Loop Assigns Inference](#loop-assigns-inference)
14	  - [Limitation](#limitation)
15	- [Additional Resources](#additional-resources)
16	
17	---
18	
19	## Syntax
20	
21	An *assigns* clause allows the user to specify a set of locations that may be assigned to by a function or the body of a loop:
22	
23	```c
24	__CPROVER_assigns(targets)
25	```
26	
27	Where `targets` has the following syntax:
28	
29	```text
30	targets           ::= cond-target-group (';' cond-target-group)* ';'?
31	cond-target-group ::= (condition ':')? target (',' target)*
32	target            ::= lvalue-expr
33	                    | __CPROVER_typed_target(lvalue-expr)
34	                    | __CPROVER_object_whole(ptr-expr)
35	                    | __CPROVER_object_from(ptr-expr)
36	                    | __CPROVER_object_upto(ptr-expr, uint-expr)
37	```
38	
39	The set of locations writable by a function is the union of the sets of locations specified by its assigns clauses, or the empty set if no *assigns* clause is specified.
40	
41	While, in general, an *assigns* clause could be interpreted with either *writes* or *modifies* semantics, this design is based on the former.
42	
43	### Lvalue targets
44	
45	Lvalue expressions designate memory locations directly and are included as targets.
46	
47	### Object slice targets
48	
49	Each target listed in an assigns clause defines a *conditionally assignable range* of bytes represented by the following triple:
50	
51	```c
52	struct {
53	  void *start_address;
54	  size_t size;
55	  bool is_writable;
56	}
57	```
58	
59	Where:
60	
61	- `start_address` is the start address of the range of bytes,
62	- `size` is the size of the range in number of bytes,
63	- `is_writable` is true iff the target's `condition` holds and `__CPROVER_w_ok(start_address, size)` holds at the program location where the clause is interpreted: right before function invocation for function contracts and at loop entry for loops.
64	
65	### Function parameters
66	
67	For a function contract, the memory locations storing function parameters are considered as being local to the function and are hence always assignable.
68	
69	For a loop contract, the parameters of the enclosing function are not considered local to the loop and must be explicitly added to the loop to become assignable.
70	
71	### Inductive data structures
72	
73	Inductive data structures are not supported yet in assigns clauses.
74	
75	---
76	
77	## Semantics
78	
79	### Contract Enforcement
80	
81	In order to determine whether a function (or loop) complies with the *assigns* clause of the contract, the body of the function (or loop) is instrumented with assertion statements before each statement which may write to memory (e.g., an assignment). These assertions check that the location about to be assigned to is among the targets specified by the *assigns* clauses.
82	
83	**Example**:
84	
85	```c
86	int sum(const uint32_t a, const uint32_t b, uint32_t* out)
87	/* Writable Set */
88	__CPROVER_assigns(*out)
89	{
90	  const uint64_t result = ((uint64_t) a) + ((uint64_t) b);
91	  *out = result;
92	  return 0;
93	}
94	```
95	
96	### Contract Replacement
97	
98	Assuming the *assigns* clause of the contract correctly captures the set of locations assigned to by a function (checked during *contract enforcement*), CBMC will use the contract’s `requires` and `ensures` clauses and its *assigns clause* to generate a sound abstraction of the function behaviour from the contract.
99	
100	Given the contract:
101	
102	```c
103	int f(params)
104	__CPROVER_requires(R);
105	__CPROVER_assigns(A);
106	__CPROVER_ensures(E);
107	{
108	  ...
109	}
110	```
111	
112	Function calls `f(args)` get replaced with a sequence of instructions equivalent to:
113	
114	```c
115	// check preconditions
116	assert(R);
117	
118	// havoc the assigns targets
119	havoc(A);
120	
121	// assume postconditions
122	assume(E);
123	```
124	
125	---
126	
127	## Loop Assigns Inference
128	
129	The inference algorithm consists of three stages:
130	
131	1. Function inlining,
132	2. Collecting assigns targets with local-may-alias analysis,
133	3. Assigns targets widening.
134	
135	We do the function inlining first so that we can infer those assigns targets hidden in the function call.
136	
137	**Example**:
138	
139	In the `test_loop_assigns_inference` example, there are five assignments in the loop:
140	
141	1. `n = &i` → assign target: `n` (loop local, excluded)
142	2. `*n++` → assign target: `*n` → alias `&i` → `i` is included
143	3. `k = i + 1` → target: `k` (loop local, excluded)
144	4. `j = i` → target: `j` → included
145	5. `b[j] = 1` → target: `b[j]` → included
146	
147	Finally, `b[j]` is widened to `__CPROVER_object_whole(b)` as its index `j` is non-constant.
148	
149	### Limitation
150	
151	The main limitation of the inference algorithm is that the local-may-alias analysis is field insensitive. For example:
152	
153	```c
154	ptr = box.ptr;
155	*ptr = 5;
156	```
157	
158	We cannot determine that `ptr` aliases `box.ptr`, and will fail to infer the assigns target `__CPROVER_object_whole(box.ptr)`.
159	
160	However, this does not result in unsoundness. CBMC will report assignability-checks failure when the inferred assigns clauses are not accurate.
161	
162	---
163	
164	## Additional Resources

169	  - Frees Clauses

172	  - Decreases Clauses

# Assigns Clauses

## Table of Contents

- [Syntax](#syntax)
  - [Lvalue targets](#lvalue-targets)
  - [Object slice targets](#object-slice-targets)
  - [Function parameters](#function-parameters)
  - [Inductive data structures](#inductive-data-structures)
- [Semantics](#semantics)
  - [Contract Enforcement](#contract-enforcement)
  - [Contract Replacement](#contract-replacement)
- [Loop Assigns Inference](#loop-assigns-inference)
  - [Limitation](#limitation)
- [Additional Resources](#additional-resources)

---

## Syntax

An *assigns* clause allows the user to specify a set of locations that may be assigned to by a function or the body of a loop:

```c
__CPROVER_assigns(targets)
```

Where `targets` has the following syntax:

```text
targets           ::= cond-target-group (';' cond-target-group)* ';'?
cond-target-group ::= (condition ':')? target (',' target)*
target            ::= lvalue-expr
                    | __CPROVER_typed_target(lvalue-expr)
                    | __CPROVER_object_whole(ptr-expr)
                    | __CPROVER_object_from(ptr-expr)
                    | __CPROVER_object_upto(ptr-expr, uint-expr)
```

The set of locations writable by a function is the union of the sets of locations specified by its assigns clauses, or the empty set if no *assigns* clause is specified.

While, in general, an *assigns* clause could be interpreted with either *writes* or *modifies* semantics, this design is based on the former.

### Lvalue targets

Lvalue expressions designate memory locations directly and are included as targets.

### Object slice targets

Each target listed in an assigns clause defines a *conditionally assignable range* of bytes represented by the following triple:

```c
struct {
  void *start_address;
  size_t size;
  bool is_writable;
}
```

Where:

- `start_address` is the start address of the range of bytes,
- `size` is the size of the range in number of bytes,
- `is_writable` is true iff the target's `condition` holds and `__CPROVER_w_ok(start_address, size)` holds at the program location where the clause is interpreted: right before function invocation for function contracts and at loop entry for loops.

### Function parameters

For a function contract, the memory locations storing function parameters are considered as being local to the function and are hence always assignable.

For a loop contract, the parameters of the enclosing function are not considered local to the loop and must be explicitly added to the loop to become assignable.

### Inductive data structures

Inductive data structures are not supported yet in assigns clauses.

---

## Semantics

### Contract Enforcement

In order to determine whether a function (or loop) complies with the *assigns* clause of the contract, the body of the function (or loop) is instrumented with assertion statements before each statement which may write to memory (e.g., an assignment). These assertions check that the location about to be assigned to is among the targets specified by the *assigns* clauses.

**Example**:

```c
int sum(const uint32_t a, const uint32_t b, uint32_t* out)
/* Writable Set */
__CPROVER_assigns(*out)
{
  const uint64_t result = ((uint64_t) a) + ((uint64_t) b);
  *out = result;
  return 0;
}
```

### Contract Replacement

Assuming the *assigns* clause of the contract correctly captures the set of locations assigned to by a function (checked during *contract enforcement*), CBMC will use the contract’s `requires` and `ensures` clauses and its *assigns clause* to generate a sound abstraction of the function behaviour from the contract.

Given the contract:

```c
int f(params)
__CPROVER_requires(R);
__CPROVER_assigns(A);
__CPROVER_ensures(E);
{
  ...
}
```

Function calls `f(args)` get replaced with a sequence of instructions equivalent to:

```c
// check preconditions
assert(R);

// havoc the assigns targets
havoc(A);

// assume postconditions
assume(E);
```

---

## Loop Assigns Inference

The inference algorithm consists of three stages:

1. Function inlining,
2. Collecting assigns targets with local-may-alias analysis,
3. Assigns targets widening.

We do the function inlining first so that we can infer those assigns targets hidden in the function call.

**Example**:

In the `test_loop_assigns_inference` example, there are five assignments in the loop:

1. `n = &i` → assign target: `n` (loop local, excluded)
2. `*n++` → assign target: `*n` → alias `&i` → `i` is included
3. `k = i + 1` → target: `k` (loop local, excluded)
4. `j = i` → target: `j` → included
5. `b[j] = 1` → target: `b[j]` → included

Finally, `b[j]` is widened to `__CPROVER_object_whole(b)` as its index `j` is non-constant.

### Limitation

The main limitation of the inference algorithm is that the local-may-alias analysis is field insensitive. For example:

```c
ptr = box.ptr;
*ptr = 5;
```

We cannot determine that `ptr` aliases `box.ptr`, and will fail to infer the assigns target `__CPROVER_object_whole(box.ptr)`.

However, this does not result in unsoundness. CBMC will report assignability-checks failure when the inferred assigns clauses are not accurate.

---

## Additional Resources

- Function Contracts
  - Requires and Ensures Clauses
  - Assigns Clauses
  - Frees Clauses
- Loop Contracts
  - Loop Invariant Clauses
  - Decreases Clauses
  - Assigns Clauses
  - Frees Clauses
- Memory Predicates
- Function Pointer Predicates
- History Variables
- Quantifiers

1	"""Run CBMC on a function.
2	
3	Usage:
4	    % run-cbmc --function <FUNCTION_NAME> \
5	                       --file <PATH_TO_C_FILE> \
6	                       [-I <PATH_TO_INCLUDE_DIR(S)>]...
7	"""
8	
9	import argparse
10	import json
11	import shlex
12	import subprocess
13	import sys
14	from dataclasses import dataclass
15	from datetime import UTC, datetime
16	from enum import StrEnum
17	from pathlib import Path
18	from subprocess import TimeoutExpired
19	
20	from tools.construct_call_graph import construct_call_graph
21	from tools.util import (
22	    build_stub_index,
23	    get_in_file_callees_for,
24	    get_stub_paths_for,
25	    get_unstubbed_external_callees_for,
26	)
27	from tools.util.callgraph import CallGraph
28	
29	# Char budget for failure responses. The harness persists full tool output to a file but
30	# previews only the head inline, so we keep responses bounded (a pathological CBMC run can
31	# emit hundreds of MB) and lead with the FAILURE lines (see `_format_failure_response`) so
32	# the verdict survives that head-only preview rather than being buried in the stdout tail.
33	_MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS = 100_000
34	
35	# Of the budget left after the header, the leading FAILURE lines, and section labels, this
36	# fraction is given to the stdout tail; the remainder goes to the stderr tail.
37	_STDOUT_TAIL_SHARE = 0.7
38	
39	_DISABLE_MACRO_FLAGS = [
40	    "-D__NO_CTYPE"  # for ctype.h
41	]
42	
43	# 10-minute timeout.
44	_DEFAULT_RUN_CBMC_TIMEOUT_SEC = 600
45	
46	# goto-cc is fast; if it hasn't finished in this many seconds, the input is almost
47	# certainly pathological and should be treated as uncompilable.
48	_GOTO_CC_TIMEOUT_SEC = 30
49	
50	# GNU `timeout(1)` convention: distinguishes a timeout from a CBMC verification failure
51	# both in the CLI exit code and in the JSONL invocation log.
52	_TIMEOUT_RETURNCODE = 124
53	
54	# Fixed values for CBMC's `--depth` and `--unwind` flags.
55	_CBMC_UNWIND = 5
56	_CBMC_DEPTH = 200

59	class CbmcStep(StrEnum):
60	    """Logical step in the CBMC verification pipeline.
61	
62	    `run_cbmc` runs the pipeline as three logical steps; each maps to one or more
63	    underlying subprocess invocations. When a step fails, the result names the step
64	    so callers can distinguish e.g. a goto-cc compile rejection from a cbmc
65	    verification failure.
66	    """
67	
68	    GOTO_CC = "goto-cc"
69	    GOTO_INSTRUMENT = "goto-instrument"
70	    CBMC = "cbmc"
71

73	@dataclass(frozen=True)
74	class RunCbmcResult:
75	    """The outcome of running CBMC on a function.
76	
77	    Attributes:
78	        function (str): The function under verification.
79	        failed_step (CbmcStep | None): The pipeline step that failed, or None on success.
80	        timed_out (bool): True iff any step hit the per-attempt timeout.
81	        returncode (int): 0 on success, `_TIMEOUT_RETURNCODE` on timeout, otherwise the
82	            exit code of the failing subprocess (cbmc's verification exit code when
83	            `failed_step` is CBMC).
84	        response (str): Printable summary — a success line or a truncated failure block.
85	    """
86	
87	    function: str
88	    failed_step: CbmcStep | None
89	    timed_out: bool
90	    returncode: int
91	    response: str
92	
93	    @property
94	    def cbmc_ran_successfully(self) -> bool:
95	        """True iff the full pipeline ran without any step failing or timing out.
96	
97	        Note: This is *not* equivalent to a successful verification result. See
98	        `is_function_verified`.
99	
100	        """
101	        return self.failed_step is None and not self.timed_out
102	
103	    @property
104	    def is_function_verified(self) -> bool:
105	        """True iff the function for this verification run is successfully verified.
106	
107	        A function is successfully verified if all components of the CBMC pipeline ran successfully
108	        and the return code is 0.
109	        """
110	        return self.cbmc_ran_successfully and self.returncode == 0
111	
112	    def __str__(self) -> str:
113	        """Return the string representation of this result, used for logging.
114	
115	        Returns:
116	            str: The string representation of this result, used for logging.
117	        """
118	        if failed_step := self.failed_step:
119	            return f"{failed_step.value.upper()}_FAILED"
120	        if self.timed_out:
121	            return "TIMED_OUT"
122	        return "PASS" if self.is_function_verified else "FAIL"
123

127	    """Outcome of running one subprocess step.
128	
129	    Attributes:
130	        step (CbmcStep): The logical step in the CBMC verification pipeline this subprocess belongs
131	            to.
132	        command (str): The shell command that was run.
133	        returncode (int): The subprocess exit code, or `_TIMEOUT_RETURNCODE` on timeout.
134	        stdout (str): Captured stdout (empty on timeout).
135	        stderr (str): Captured stderr (empty on timeout).
136	        timed_out (bool): True iff the subprocess hit the per-step timeout.
137	    """
138	
139	    step: CbmcStep
140	    command: str
141	    returncode: int
142	    stdout: str
143	    stderr: str
144	    timed_out: bool
145	
146	    @property
147	    def succeeded(self) -> bool:
148	        """True iff the subprocess exited zero and did not time out."""
149	        return self.returncode == 0 and not self.timed_out

153	    """Run CBMC on a function."""
154	    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
155	        description=(
156	            "Run CBMC on a function with loop unwinding = _CBMC_UNWIND, depth = _CBMC_DEPTH. "
157	            "Exits with status 0 on verification success."
158	        )
159	    )
160	    parser.add_argument("--function", required=True, help="Name of the function to verify.")
161	    parser.add_argument("--file", required=True, help="Path to the C file defining the function.")
162	    parser.add_argument(
163	        "-I",
164	        "--include-dir",
165	        action="append",

167	        dest="include_dirs",
168	        metavar="DIR",

170	    )

172	    result = run_cbmc(

181	def run_cbmc(
182	    function_to_verify: str,
183	    file_containing_function_to_verify: str,
184	    include_dirs: list[str] | None = None,
185	    call_graph: CallGraph | None = None,
186	    cwd: str | None = None,
187	) -> RunCbmcResult:
188	    """Run CBMC on the given function with loop unwinding = 5, depth = 100.
189	
190	    The pipeline is split into three logical steps — `goto-cc`, `goto-instrument`, and
191	    `cbmc` — each run as its own subprocess so that failures can be attributed to a
192	    specific step. Up to three pipeline attempts are made: an initial run, a retry
193	    when an apparent recursive-inlining error is detected, and a retry when a missing
194	    callee body is detected. The timeout is per CBMC subprocess attempt, not a total
195	    budget; a timeout in any single step terminates the whole call.
196	
197	    Args:
198	        function_to_verify (str): Name of the function to verify.
199	        file_containing_function_to_verify (str): Path to the C file defining the function.
200	        include_dirs (list[str] | None): Directories to add to the C preprocessor's include
201	            search path. Forwarded to `goto-cc` as `-I` flags.
202	        call_graph (CallGraph | None): When provided, this pre-built call graph is used instead
203	            of parsing `file_containing_function_to_verify`.
204	        cwd (str | None): Working directory for every subprocess in the pipeline. The pipeline
205	            writes its intermediate `<function>.goto` / `checking-<function>-contracts.goto` files
206	            relative to this directory, so concurrent runs of the same function must each pass a
207	            distinct `cwd` to avoid clobbering one another. When None, subprocesses inherit the
208	            current working directory.
209	
210	    Returns:
211	        RunCbmcResult: The outcome of the run, naming the failed step (if any) and carrying
212	            a printable response and the relevant exit code.
213	    """
214	    if call_graph is None:
215	        path_to_raw_call_graph = construct_call_graph(file_containing_function_to_verify)
216	        call_graph = CallGraph(json.loads(Path(path_to_raw_call_graph).read_text(encoding="utf-8")))
217	    callees = get_in_file_callees_for(function_to_verify, call_graph)
218	    # Building the stub index is inexpensive for now (there is a single file).
219	    # Re-visit this if/when we have more stub files to parse.
220	    stub_index = build_stub_index()
221	    stub_paths = get_stub_paths_for(function_to_verify, call_graph, stub_index)
222	    nondet_callees = get_unstubbed_external_callees_for(function_to_verify, call_graph, stub_index)
223	
224	    step_records: list[dict] = []
225	
226	    # Initial attempt.
227	    result, combined_stdout, combined_stderr = _run_pipeline(
228	        function_to_verify,
229	        callees,
230	        file_containing_function_to_verify,
231	        stub_paths=stub_paths,
232	        include_dirs=include_dirs,
233	        prevent_macro_expansion=False,
234	        step_records=step_records,
235	        cwd=cwd,
236	    )
237	    # First, check if the run was successful or if it timed out.
238	    if result.cbmc_ran_successfully or result.timed_out:
239	        _log_invocation(file_containing_function_to_verify, result, step_records, nondet_callees)
240	        return result
241	
242	    # Recursion-inlining retry.
243	    if has_recursion_inlining_error_message(function_to_verify, combined_stdout, combined_stderr):
244	        callees = get_in_file_callees_for(
245	            function_to_verify,
246	            call_graph,
247	            include_self=call_graph.is_self_recursive(function_to_verify),
248	        )
249	        result, combined_stdout, combined_stderr = _run_pipeline(
250	            function_to_verify,
251	            callees,
252	            file_containing_function_to_verify,
253	            stub_paths=stub_paths,
254	            include_dirs=include_dirs,
255	            prevent_macro_expansion=False,
256	            step_records=step_records,
257	            cwd=cwd,
258	        )
259	
260	    # Missing-body retry: re-run with macro expansion suppressed.
261	    if not result.cbmc_ran_successfully and has_missing_body_for_callee_or_function_message(
262	        combined_stdout, combined_stderr
263	    ):
264	        result, combined_stdout, combined_stderr = _run_pipeline(
265	            function_to_verify,
266	            callees,
267	            file_containing_function_to_verify,
268	            stub_paths=stub_paths,
269	            include_dirs=include_dirs,
270	            prevent_macro_expansion=True,
271	            step_records=step_records,
272	            cwd=cwd,
273	        )
274	
275	    _log_invocation(file_containing_function_to_verify, result, step_records, nondet_callees)
276	    return result
277

279	def _run_pipeline(
280	    function_to_verify: str,
281	    callees: list[str],
282	    file_containing_function: str,
283	    stub_paths: list[str] | None,
284	    include_dirs: list[str] | None,
285	    prevent_macro_expansion: bool,
286	    step_records: list[dict],
287	    cwd: str | None = None,
288	) -> tuple[RunCbmcResult, str, str]:
289	    """Run the goto-cc → goto-instrument → cbmc pipeline once.
290	
291	    Each subprocess is run separately so the first failure (or timeout) can be attributed
292	    to its logical step. Step records are appended to `step_records` for the JSONL log.
293	
294	    Args:
295	        function_to_verify (str): The function under verification.
296	        callees (list[str]): Callees of the function, used by `--replace-call-with-contract`.
297	        file_containing_function (str): The C source file containing the function.
298	        stub_paths (list[str] | None): Extra `.c` stub files compiled in alongside the source.
299	        include_dirs (list[str] | None): Directories forwarded to `goto-cc` as `-I` flags.
300	        prevent_macro_expansion (bool): When True, disable macros CBMC can't model and inject
301	            the bundled C-library models before contract enforcement.
302	        step_records (list[dict]): Mutated in place — one record per subprocess invocation
303	            appended in order. Used by `_log_invocation` to produce the JSONL row.
304	        cwd (str | None): Working directory for every subprocess, forwarded to `_run_step`. The
305	            pipeline's intermediate `.goto` files are written relative to this directory.
306	
307	    Returns:
308	        tuple[RunCbmcResult, str, str]: The result of the pipeline plus the concatenated
309	            stdout and stderr across every step that ran. The concatenated output is used by
310	            the retry triggers in `run_cbmc`.
311	    """
312	    commands: list[tuple[CbmcStep, str]] = [
313	        (
314	            CbmcStep.GOTO_CC,
315	            _get_goto_cc_command(
316	                function_to_verify,
317	                file_containing_function,
318	                stub_paths=stub_paths,
319	                include_dirs=include_dirs,
320	                prevent_macro_expansion=prevent_macro_expansion,
321	            ),
322	        ),
323	    ]
324	    if prevent_macro_expansion:
325	        commands.append(
326	            (
327	                CbmcStep.GOTO_INSTRUMENT,
328	                _get_goto_instrument_add_library_command(function_to_verify),
329	            )
330	        )
331	    commands.extend(
332	        [
333	            (
334	                CbmcStep.GOTO_INSTRUMENT,
335	                _get_goto_instrument_unwind_command(function_to_verify),
336	            ),
337	            (
338	                CbmcStep.GOTO_INSTRUMENT,
339	                _get_goto_instrument_contract_command(function_to_verify, callees),
340	            ),
341	            (CbmcStep.CBMC, _get_cbmc_check_command(function_to_verify)),
342	        ]
343	    )
344	
345	    combined_stdout = ""
346	    combined_stderr = ""
347	    for step, command in commands:
348	        step_run = _run_step(step, command, cwd=cwd)
349	        step_records.append(
350	            {
351	                "step": step.value,
352	                "command": step_run.command,
353	                "returncode": step_run.returncode,
354	            }
355	        )
356	        combined_stdout += step_run.stdout
357	        combined_stderr += step_run.stderr
358	        if not step_run.succeeded:
359	            return (
360	                _result_from_failure(
361	                    function_to_verify, step_run, combined_stdout, combined_stderr
362	                ),
363	                combined_stdout,
364	                combined_stderr,
365	            )
366	
367	    return (
368	        RunCbmcResult(
369	            function=function_to_verify,
370	            failed_step=None,
371	            timed_out=False,
372	            returncode=0,
373	            response=f"{function_to_verify} verified successfully",
374	        ),
375	        combined_stdout,
376	        combined_stderr,
377	    )
378	
379	
380	def _run_step(step: CbmcStep, command: str, cwd: str | None = None) -> _StepRun:
381	    """Run one pipeline step as a subprocess with the per-step timeout.
382	
383	    Args:
384	        step (CbmcStep): The logical step this subprocess belongs to.
385	        command (str): The shell command to run.
386	        cwd (str | None): Working directory for the subprocess. When None, the subprocess
387	            inherits the current working directory. Distinct directories let concurrent
388	            pipelines for the same function avoid clobbering each other's `.goto` files.
389	
390	    Returns:
391	        _StepRun: The captured outcome, including stdout/stderr or the timeout sentinel.
392	    """
393	    try:
394	        completed = subprocess.run(
395	            command,
396	            capture_output=True,
397	            text=True,
398	            shell=True,
399	            check=False,
400	            timeout=_DEFAULT_RUN_CBMC_TIMEOUT_SEC,
401	            cwd=cwd,
402	        )
403	    except TimeoutExpired:
404	        return _StepRun(
405	            step=step,
406	            command=command,
407	            returncode=_TIMEOUT_RETURNCODE,
408	            stdout="",
409	            stderr="",
410	            timed_out=True,
411	        )
412	    return _StepRun(
413	        step=step,
414	        command=command,
415	        returncode=completed.returncode,
416	        stdout=completed.stdout,
417	        stderr=completed.stderr,
418	        timed_out=False,
419	    )
420

422	def _result_from_failure(
423	    function: str, step_run: _StepRun, combined_stdout: str, combined_stderr: str
424	) -> RunCbmcResult:
425	    """Build a `RunCbmcResult` for a pipeline that failed at `step_run`.
426	
427	    Args:
428	        function (str): The function under verification.
429	        step_run (_StepRun): The step that failed or timed out.
430	        combined_stdout (str): Concatenated stdout across every step that ran (including
431	            the failing one). Used to render the failure response.
432	        combined_stderr (str): Concatenated stderr across every step that ran.
433	
434	    Returns:
435	        RunCbmcResult: A failure result whose `response` is a formatted, truncated block.
436	    """
437	    if step_run.timed_out:
438	        response = (
439	            f"Verification for '{function}' timed out after "
440	            f"{_DEFAULT_RUN_CBMC_TIMEOUT_SEC} second(s) during {step_run.step.value}"
441	        )
442	        return RunCbmcResult(
443	            function=function,
444	            failed_step=step_run.step,
445	            timed_out=True,
446	            returncode=_TIMEOUT_RETURNCODE,
447	            response=response,
448	        )
449	    response = _format_failure_response(function, step_run.step, combined_stdout, combined_stderr)
450	    return RunCbmcResult(
451	        function=function,
452	        failed_step=step_run.step,
453	        timed_out=False,
454	        returncode=step_run.returncode,
455	        response=response,
456	    )

459	def has_recursion_inlining_error_message(function: str, stdout: str, stderr: str) -> bool:
460	    """Return True iff CBMC reports an error with inlining given function, which might be recursive.
461	
462	    Args:
463	        function (str): The function that might be recursive.
464	        stdout (str): The stdout of a CBMC command.
465	        stderr (str): The stderr of a CBMC command.
466	
467	    Returns:
468	        bool: True iff CBMC reports an error with inlining given function, which might be recursive.
469	    """
470	    recursion_error_message = f"Recursive call to '{function}' during inlining"
471	    return recursion_error_message in stdout or recursion_error_message in stderr

474	def has_missing_body_for_callee_or_function_message(stdout: str, stderr: str) -> bool:
475	    """Return True iff CBMC output indicates a callee or function body is missing.
476	
477	    The CBMC error output contains the string "no body for callee" when a callee of a function under
478	    verification is missing its body.
479	
480	    It contains the string "no body for function" in cases where a function body may be missing
481	    (e.g., when a stub file is not passed in).
482	
483	    In this case, it doesn't hurt to re-run CBMC while suppressing
484	    macro expansion (e.g., `isspace` in ctype.h expands to `__ctype_loc`, which CBMC lacks a model
485	    for).
486	
487	    Args:
488	        stdout (str): The stdout of a CBMC command.
489	        stderr (str): The stderr of a CBMC command.
490	
491	    Returns:
492	        bool: True iff CBMC output indicates a callee body is missing.
493	    """
494	    missing_callee_indicator = "no body for callee"
495	    missing_function_indicator = "no body for function"
496	    return (
497	        missing_callee_indicator in stdout
498	        or missing_callee_indicator in stderr
499	        or missing_function_indicator in stdout
500	        or missing_function_indicator in stderr
501	    )

504	def _format_failure_response(function: str, failed_step: CbmcStep, stdout: str, stderr: str) -> str:
505	    """Format a CBMC failure response, leading with the FAILURE lines.
506	
507	    After the header, the response always begins with the FAILURE lines extracted from
508	    stdout, so the verdict survives the harness's head-only inline preview: CBMC prints its
509	    FAILURE lines and verdict at the *tail* of stdout, which a positional truncation would
510	    otherwise bury. The full stderr and stdout follow; when the combined output exceeds
511	    `_MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS`, each stream is replaced by its tail behind a truncation marker.
512	    For `goto-cc`/`goto-instrument` failures stdout has no FAILURE lines, so the response
513	    leads with stderr, where those tools print their diagnostics.
514	
515	    Args:
516	        function (str): The name of the function that failed verification.
517	        failed_step (CbmcStep): The pipeline step that failed.
518	        stdout (str): The concatenated stdout across every step that ran.
519	        stderr (str): The concatenated stderr across every step that ran.
520	
521	    Returns:
522	        str: The formatted CBMC failure response, leading with the FAILURE lines and
523	            truncated iff it has exceeded the char budget.
524	    """
525	    if failed_step is CbmcStep.CBMC:
526	        header = f"{function} failed to verify with the following errors:\n\n"
527	    else:
528	        header = f"{function} failed at {failed_step.value} with the following errors:\n\n"
529	
530	    failure_section = _failure_lines_section(stdout)
531	
532	    # Lead with the FAILURE lines, then show both streams in full when everything fits.
533	    full = f"{header}{failure_section}--- stderr ---\n{stderr}\n--- stdout ---\n{stdout}"
534	    if len(full) <= _MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS:
535	        return full
536	
537	    # Over budget: keep the leading FAILURE lines and replace each stream with its tail.
538	    # The `_MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS`-wide placeholder pads the marker's digit count so the
539	    # actual marker (with the real dropped count) cannot push us over budget.
540	    digit_pad = str(_MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS)
541	    marker = f"[... {digit_pad} characters truncated ...]\n"
542	    fixed = (
543	        f"{header}{failure_section}--- stderr (tail) ---\n{marker}--- stdout (tail) ---\n{marker}"
544	    )
545	    remaining = max(_MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS - len(fixed), 0)
546	    stdout_budget = int(remaining * _STDOUT_TAIL_SHARE)
547	    stderr_budget = remaining - stdout_budget
548	
549	    stderr_section = _tail_section("stderr (tail)", stderr, stderr_budget)
550	    stdout_section = _tail_section("stdout (tail)", stdout, stdout_budget)
551	
552	    response = f"{header}{failure_section}{stderr_section}{stdout_section}"
553	    # Hard clamp: the per-section budget accounting can drift by a few chars
554	    # against the `fixed` estimate, so guarantee we never exceed the cap.
555	    return response[:_MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS]
556	
557	
558	def _failure_lines_section(stdout: str) -> str:
559	    """Render the leading "FAILURE lines" section of a failure response, or "" if none.
560	
561	    Extracts the FAILURE lines from `stdout` and renders them as a labeled section, capped
562	    at half the total budget so a pathological run with tens of thousands of FAILURE lines
563	    can't blow past the limit on its own. Returns the empty string when stdout has no
564	    FAILURE lines (e.g. a goto-cc compile failure), so callers naturally lead with stderr.
565	
566	    Args:
567	        stdout (str): The concatenated stdout across every step that ran.
568	
569	    Returns:
570	        str: The labeled FAILURE-lines section ending in a newline, or "" if there are none.
571	    """
572	    failure_lines = [line for line in stdout.split("\n") if "FAILURE" in line]
573	    if not failure_lines:
574	        return ""
575	    failure_block = "\n".join(failure_lines)
576	    failure_cap = _MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS // 2
577	    if len(failure_block) > failure_cap:
578	        dropped = len(failure_block) - failure_cap
579	        failure_block = (
580	            f"[... {dropped} characters of FAILURE lines truncated ...]\n"
581	            f"{failure_block[-failure_cap:]}"
582	        )
583	    return f"--- FAILURE lines ---\n{failure_block}\n"
584	
585	
586	def _tail_section(label: str, content: str, budget: int) -> str:
587	    """Render a labeled section containing the tail of content within budget chars.
588	
589	    Args:
590	        label (str): The label of the section.
591	        content (str): The content to truncate.
592	        budget (int): The maximum number of characters to include in the section.
593	
594	    Returns:
595	        str: The labeled section containing the tail of content within budget chars.
596	    """
597	    if len(content) <= budget:
598	        body = content
599	    else:
600	        dropped = len(content) - budget
601	        body = f"[... {dropped} characters truncated ...]\n{content[-budget:]}"
602	    return f"--- {label} ---\n{body}\n"
603

605	def _log_invocation(
606	    file_under_verification: str,
607	    result: RunCbmcResult,
608	    step_records: list[dict],
609	    nondet_callees: list[str],
610	) -> None:
611	    """Log a CBMC invocation with the given arguments.
612	
613	    Args:
614	        file_under_verification (str): The file that contains the function under verification.
615	        result (RunCbmcResult): The final outcome of the `run_cbmc` call.
616	        step_records (list[dict]): Per-subprocess records (one entry per step invocation across
617	            every pipeline attempt), each with keys `step`, `command`, `returncode`.
618	        nondet_callees (list[str]): The list of callees that CBMC treated as non-deterministic
619	            during verification.
620	    """
621	    source_path = Path(file_under_verification)
622	    log_path = source_path.with_name(f"{source_path.stem}-cbmc-runs.jsonl")
623	    record = {
624	        "ts": datetime.now(UTC).strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"),
625	        "function": result.function,
626	        "file": file_under_verification,
627	        "failed_step": result.failed_step.value if result.failed_step is not None else None,
628	        "returncode": result.returncode,
629	        "timed_out": result.timed_out,
630	        "steps": step_records,
631	        "nondet_callees": nondet_callees,
632	    }
633	    try:
634	        with log_path.open("a") as f:
635	            f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")
636	    except OSError:
637	        # This does fail silently, but it shouldn't stop the tool from making progress.
638	        pass
639	
640	
641	def _get_goto_cc_command(
642	    function: str,
643	    file_containing_function: str,
644	    stub_paths: list[str] | None = None,
645	    include_dirs: list[str] | None = None,
646	    prevent_macro_expansion: bool = False,
647	) -> str:
648	    """Return the goto-cc command that compiles `file_containing_function` to a goto-binary.
649	
650	    This is the first step of the CBMC pipeline, exposed as a standalone helper so
651	    callers can run only the compile phase — useful for cheaply detecting inputs that goto-cc
652	    rejects (e.g., a body-mutated source where mutating `p - q` to `p + q` is illegal C).
653	
654	    Args:
655	        function (str): The function used as the goto-binary entry point.
656	        file_containing_function (str): Path to the C source file.
657	        stub_paths (list[str] | None): Extra `.c` stub files compiled in alongside the source.
658	        include_dirs (list[str] | None): Directories forwarded as `-I` flags.
659	        prevent_macro_expansion (bool): If True, disable macros that CBMC cannot model.
660	
661	    Returns:
662	        str: A single shell command suitable for `subprocess.run(..., shell=True)`.
663	    """
664	    quoted_function = shlex.quote(function)
665	    flags_disabling_macro_expansion = (
666	        f"{' '.join(_DISABLE_MACRO_FLAGS)} " if prevent_macro_expansion else ""
667	    )
668	    extra_stub_args = f" {' '.join(shlex.quote(p) for p in stub_paths)}" if stub_paths else ""
669	    include_flags = "".join(f" -I {shlex.quote(d)}" for d in include_dirs) if include_dirs else ""
670	    return (
671	        f"goto-cc {flags_disabling_macro_expansion}-o {quoted_function}.goto"
672	        f"{include_flags} "
673	        f"{shlex.quote(file_containing_function)}{extra_stub_args} "
674	        f"--function {quoted_function}"
675	    )
676	
677	
678	def _get_goto_instrument_add_library_command(function: str) -> str:
679	    """Return the `goto-instrument --add-library` command for `function`'s goto-binary.
680	
681	    This step injects CBMC's bundled C-library models (`isspace`, `strchr`, etc.) into
682	    the goto-binary before contract enforcement runs. It's only used when macro expansion
683	    is suppressed; without it, those calls are treated as nondeterministic, which loosens
684	    precision and can cause `goto-instrument` to emit "no body for function" warnings.
685	
686	    Args:
687	        function (str): The function whose goto-binary should receive the library models.
688	
689	    Returns:
690	        str: A single shell command.
691	    """
692	    quoted_function = shlex.quote(function)
693	    return f"goto-instrument --add-library {quoted_function}.goto {quoted_function}.goto"
694	
695	
696	def _get_goto_instrument_unwind_command(function: str) -> str:
697	    """Return the `goto-instrument --partial-loops --unwind _CBMC_UNWIND` command for `function`.
698	
699	    Args:
700	        function (str): The function whose goto-binary should be unwound.
701	
702	    Returns:
703	        str: A single shell command.
704	    """
705	    quoted_function = shlex.quote(function)
706	    return (
707	        f"goto-instrument --partial-loops "
708	        f"--unwind {_CBMC_UNWIND} {quoted_function}.goto {quoted_function}.goto"
709	    )
710	
711	
712	def _get_goto_instrument_contract_command(function: str, callees: list[str]) -> str:
713	    """Return the `goto-instrument --enforce-contract` command for `function`.
714	
715	    Args:
716	        function (str): The function whose contract should be enforced.
717	        callees (list[str]): Callees whose contracts should be substituted for the call sites
718	            via `--replace-call-with-contract`.
719	
720	    Returns:
721	        str: A single shell command.
722	    """
723	    quoted_function = shlex.quote(function)
724	    replace_calls = "".join(f" --replace-call-with-contract {shlex.quote(c)}" for c in callees)
725	    return (
726	        f"goto-instrument{replace_calls} "
727	        f"--enforce-contract {quoted_function} "
728	        f"{quoted_function}.goto "
729	        f"checking-{quoted_function}-contracts.goto"
730	    )

732

734	    """Return the final `cbmc` command that checks `function`'s instrumented goto-binary.
735	
736	    Args:
737	        function (str): The function under verification.
738	
739	    Returns:
740	        str: A single shell command.
741	    """
742	    quoted_function = shlex.quote(function)
743	    return (
744	        f"cbmc checking-{quoted_function}-contracts.goto "
745	        f"--function {quoted_function} --depth {_CBMC_DEPTH}"
746	    )

749	def compile_with_goto_cc(
750	    function: str,
751	    file_path: str,
752	    include_dirs: list[str] | None = None,
753	) -> int:
754	    """Run only the goto-cc compile step on a C file and return its exit code.
755	
756	    A zero exit code means goto-cc accepted the input; non-zero means it rejected it.
757

759	        function (str): The function used as the goto-binary entry point.
760	        file_path (str): Path to the C file to compile.
761	        include_dirs (list[str] | None): Directories forwarded as `-I` flags.
762

765	    """
766	    compilation_command = _get_goto_cc_command(function, file_path, include_dirs=include_dirs)
767	    try:

769	            compilation_command,
770	            capture_output=True,

772	            shell=True,
773	            check=False,
